subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Four particles $A, B, C, D$ of mass $\frac{m}{2}, m, 2 m, 4 m$, have same momentum, respectively. The particle with maximum kinetic energy is :
question: Four particles $A, B, C, D$ of mass $\frac{m}{2}, m, 2 m, 4 m$, have same momentum, respectively. The particle with maximum kinetic energy is :
solution:
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
Same momentum, so less mass means more KE. So $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}$ will have max. $\mathrm{KE}$.

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> rests on a vertical post of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, travelling in horizontal direction, hits the centre of the ball. After collision both travels independently. The ball hits the ground at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the bullet at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the foot of the post. The value of initial velocity of the bullet will be (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>) :
question: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> rests on a vertical post of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, travelling in horizontal direction, hits the centre of the ball. After collision both travels independently. The ball hits the ground at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the bullet at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the foot of the post. The value of initial velocity of the bullet will be (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>) :
solution:
Let initial velocity of bullet be u.

Given, mass of bullet m=10 g and mass of ball M=200 g,

Initially ball is at height 5 m and at rest, the only acceleration acting is due to gravity.

Time of flight of each ball and bullet is t=2hg.

Now, velocity of ball and bullet as v1=302hg and v2=1202hg

As the collision is elastic, so applying conservation of linear momentum.

mu+M0=Mv1+mv2

⇒0.01u=0.230g2h+0.01120g2h

⇒u=300+60=360 m s-1

q: The centre of mass of a solid hemisphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre of the flat surface. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
question: The centre of mass of a solid hemisphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre of the flat surface. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
Centre of Mass of a solid hemisphere from centre of the  flat surface is given by,hcm=3R8

It is given that R= 8 cm

Therefore,

 x=hcm=3R8=3×88x=3 cm

q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving horizontally with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> extra mass is added, the final velocity (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) is:
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving horizontally with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> extra mass is added, the final velocity (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) is:
solution:
From the conservation of linear momentum, it can be written that

Mu=M+mv   ...1

where, M is the initial mass, u is the initial velocity, m is the extra mass added and v is the final velocity of the composition.

From equation (1), it follows that

1000×6=1200×v⇒v=5 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three bodies A, B and C have equal kinetic energies and their masses are $400 \mathrm{~g}$. $1.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $1.6 \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively. The ratio of their linear momenta is :
question: Three bodies A, B and C have equal kinetic energies and their masses are $400 \mathrm{~g}$. $1.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $1.6 \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively. The ratio of their linear momenta is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & \mathrm{P} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}: \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}: \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}}$
$=\sqrt{400}: \sqrt{1200}: \sqrt{1600}=1: \sqrt{3}: 2$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> makes an elastic collision with a second body at rest and continues to move in the original direction but with one fourth of its original speed. What is the mass of the second body?
question: A body of mass <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> makes an elastic collision with a second body at rest and continues to move in the original direction but with one fourth of its original speed. What is the mass of the second body?
solution:
From conservation of linear momentum
mU=mU4+MV . . . (i)
For elastic collision: e=1=V-U4u
V=5U4 . . . (ii)
From equation (i) & (ii),
mU=mU4+M5U4
m=m4+54M  ⇒M=3m5=1.2 kg

q: The distance of centre of mass from end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> of a one dimensional rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> having mass density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> (in meter) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>L</mi></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _____ (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is the distance form end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>)
question: The distance of centre of mass from end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> of a one dimensional rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> having mass density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> (in meter) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>L</mi></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _____ (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is the distance form end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>)
solution:
Given here:

Mass density, ρ=ρ01-x2L2 kg m-1

Let us suppose there is an element of length dx at a distance x from left must end.

Mass of this element is dm=ρdx=ρ01-x2L2

The centre of mass of this element is Xcm=∫xdm∫dm

=ρ0∫0lx-x2L2xdx∫0lρ01-x2L2dx

=L22-L44L2L-L33L2=L242L3=3L8

Hence, the value of α=8.

q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R). Assertion (A) :
Three identical spheres of same mass undergo one dimensional motion as shown in figure with initial velocities $v_{\mathrm{A}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If we wait sufficiently long for elastic collision to happen, then $v_{\mathrm{A}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, $v_{\mathrm{C}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ will be the final velocities. Reason (R): In an elastic collision between identical masses, two objects exchange their velocities. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R). Assertion (A) :
Three identical spheres of same mass undergo one dimensional motion as shown in figure with initial velocities $v_{\mathrm{A}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If we wait sufficiently long for elastic collision to happen, then $v_{\mathrm{A}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, $v_{\mathrm{C}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ will be the final velocities. Reason (R): In an elastic collision between identical masses, two objects exchange their velocities. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)

q: The identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of these two sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of the centre of mass of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________.
question: The identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of these two sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of the centre of mass of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________.
solution:
Position vector r→COM=m1r→1+m2r→2+m3r→3 m1+m2+m3

r→COM =2M×0+2M×4i^+2M×4j^6M

⇒r→=43i^+43j^

|r→|=432+432=423

Therefore, x=3.

question: A circular hole of radius a2 is cut out of a circular disc of radius a as shown in figure. The centroid of the remaining circular portion with respect to point O will be:
solution:

xCOM=σπa2a-σπa24×3a2σπa2-σπa24

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext mathvariant="italic">m</mtext></math> moving in the <math><mi>x</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mn>2</mn><mi>v</mi></math> is hit by another particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> moving in the <math><mi>y</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mi>v</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the collision is perfectly inelastic, the percentage loss in the energy during the collision is close to:
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext mathvariant="italic">m</mtext></math> moving in the <math><mi>x</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mn>2</mn><mi>v</mi></math> is hit by another particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> moving in the <math><mi>y</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mi>v</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the collision is perfectly inelastic, the percentage loss in the energy during the collision is close to:
solution:
The initial momentum of system is Pi→=m2Vi^+2mV j^

According to question as



On perfectly inelastic collision the particles stick to each other.

Pf→=3m Vf→

By conservation of linear momentum

Pf→=Pi→ ⇒3m Vf→=m2V i^+2mVj^

⇒Vf→=2V3i^+j^⇒ | V f | =223V

∴ loss in KE. of system =Kinitial-Kfinal

=12m2V2+122mV2-123m 2 2 V⁡ 3 2

=2mV2+mV2-43mV2=3mV2-4mV23=53mV2

% Loss in KE =100×∆KKi=53mv23mV2=59×100=56%

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three masses $\mathrm{m}, 2 \mathrm{~m}$ and $3 \mathrm{~m}$ are moving in $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}$ plane with speed $3 \mathrm{u}, 2 \mathrm{u}$ and $\mathrm{u}$ respectively as shown in figure. The three masses collide at the same point at $\mathrm{P}$ and stick together. The velocity of resulting mass will be:
question: Three masses $\mathrm{m}, 2 \mathrm{~m}$ and $3 \mathrm{~m}$ are moving in $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}$ plane with speed $3 \mathrm{u}, 2 \mathrm{u}$ and $\mathrm{u}$ respectively as shown in figure. The three masses collide at the same point at $\mathrm{P}$ and stick together. The velocity of resulting mass will be:
solution:
From the law of conservation of momentum we know that, $$ \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{u}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{u}_2+\ldots .=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2+\ldots . $$ Given $\mathrm{m}_1=\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{m}_2=2 \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{m}_3=3 \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{u}_1=3 \mathrm{u}, \mathrm{u}_2=2 \mathrm{u}$ and $\mathrm{u}_3=\mathrm{u}$ Let the velocity when they stick $=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ $$ \text { Then, according to question, } $$
$$ \begin{aligned} &m \times 3 u(\hat{i})+2 m \times 2 u\left(-\hat{i} \cos 60^{\circ}-\hat{j} \sin 60^{\circ}\right) \\ &+3 \mathrm{~m} \times \mathrm{u}\left(-\hat{\mathrm{i}} \cos 60^{\circ}+\hat{\mathrm{j}} \sin 60^{\circ}\right) \\ &=(\mathrm{m}+2 \mathrm{~m}+3 \mathrm{~m}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \\ &\Rightarrow 3 m u \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \mathrm{mu} \frac{\hat{\mathrm{i}}}{2}-4 \mathrm{mu}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right)-3 \mathrm{mu} \frac{\hat{\mathrm{i}}}{2} \\ &+3 m u\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right)=6 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{v} \\ &\Rightarrow m u \hat{i}-\frac{3}{2} m u \hat{i}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m \hat{j}=6 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{v} \\ &\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{2} m u \hat{i}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m u \hat{j}=6 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{v} \\ &\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{12}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\sqrt{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \\ & \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Using relative velocity



arel=0

vrel=100

Time t for collision,

100=vrel×t

t=100100=1 sec

vbullet=100-1×10=90m/s

vparticle=10×1=10m/s

S=100×1-12×10×1=95 m



Pi=Pf

⇒90×0.02-10×0.03=0.05V

V=30m/s⇒h=v22g=90020=45 m

So from top of building

45-5=40 m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
question:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
options: Impulse and average force acting on the object will be same for both the cases.
Impulse will be same for both the cases but the average force will be different.
Average force will be same for both the cases but the impulse will be different.
Average force and impulse will be different for both the cases.
solution:
Impulse = Change in momentum

I=ΔP

Therefore, in both the cases the impulse is same.

Average force is defined as,

Favg =ΔPΔt

Since the time in the two cases is different, the average force will be different.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: In a system two particles of masses $m_1=3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ are placed at certain distance from each other. The particle of mass $m_1$ is moved towards the center of mass of the system through a distance $2 \mathrm{~cm}$. In order to keep the center of mass of the system at the original position, the particle of mass $m_2$ should move towards the center of mass by the distance _____ $\mathrm{cm}$.
question: In a system two particles of masses $m_1=3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ are placed at certain distance from each other. The particle of mass $m_1$ is moved towards the center of mass of the system through a distance $2 \mathrm{~cm}$. In order to keep the center of mass of the system at the original position, the particle of mass $m_2$ should move towards the center of mass by the distance _____ $\mathrm{cm}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{X}_{\text {C.O.M. }}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_1 \Delta \mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \Delta \mathrm{x}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2} \\ & \Rightarrow 0=\frac{3 \times 2+2(-\mathrm{x})}{3+2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=3 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A man (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) and his son (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) are standing on a frictionless surface facing each other. The man pushes his son so that he starts moving at a speed of <math><mn>0.70</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> with respect to the man. The speed of the man with respect to the surface is:
question: A man (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) and his son (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) are standing on a frictionless surface facing each other. The man pushes his son so that he starts moving at a speed of <math><mn>0.70</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> with respect to the man. The speed of the man with respect to the surface is:
solution:
By conservation of linear momentum,

P→i=P→f

Where P→i and P→f are initial and final momentum of system (boy + man)

Now,  0=mbv→b+mmv→m......(i)

vb= velocity of boy w.r.t. ground

vm= velocity of man w.r.t. ground

v→bm= velocity of boy w.r.t. man

So, v→bm=v→b-v→m

or v→b=v→bm+v→m

or v→b=0.7+v→m ........(ii)

From equation (i) and (ii),

0=200.7+v→m+50 v→m

v→m=-1470=-0.20 m s-1

Negative sign shows velocity of man is opposite to boy.

q: Consider a circular disc of radius 20 cm with centre located at the origin. A circular hole of radius 5 cm is cut from this disc in such a way that the edge of the hole touches the edge of the disc. The distance of centre of mass of residual or remaining disc from the origin will be
question: Consider a circular disc of radius 20 cm with centre located at the origin. A circular hole of radius 5 cm is cut from this disc in such a way that the edge of the hole touches the edge of the disc. The distance of centre of mass of residual or remaining disc from the origin will be
solution: $\text { mass of disc }=\mathrm{m}$ $\text { mass of cut part }=\frac{m}{16}$ $X_{c o m}=\frac{m \times 0-\frac{m}{16} \times 15}{m-\frac{m}{16}}$ $=1 \mathrm{~cm} .$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: As per the given figure, a small ball P slides down the quadrant of a circle and hits the other ball Q of equal mass which is initially at rest. Neglecting the effect of friction and assume the collision to be elastic, the velocity of ball Q after collision will be : (g = 10 m s-2)
question: As per the given figure, a small ball P slides down the quadrant of a circle and hits the other ball Q of equal mass which is initially at rest. Neglecting the effect of friction and assume the collision to be elastic, the velocity of ball Q after collision will be : (g = 10 m s-2)
solution:
Let vp be the velocity of ball P just before collision. Therefore, applying conservation of energy for P (between the moment it is released and the moment just before collision),

mgl+0=12mvp2

⇒vp=2gl

          =2×10×0.2

         =2 m s-1 

Since both balls have equal masses and the collision is perfectly elastic, the velocities of both the balls will get interchanged.

Therefore, the velocity of ball Q just after the collision is 2 m s-1.

q: An object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> collides with another object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, which is at rest. After the collision the objects move with equal speeds in opposite direction. The ratio of the masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is :
question: An object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> collides with another object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, which is at rest. After the collision the objects move with equal speeds in opposite direction. The ratio of the masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is :
solution:
m1v1=-m1v+m2v

v1=-v+m2 m1v

v1+vv=m2 m1

e=2vv1=1

v=v12

v1+v1/2v1/2=m2 m1

3=m2 m1

q: Two billiard balls of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each moving in opposite directions with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collide and rebound with the same speed. If the time duration of contact is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>005</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, then what is the force exerted on the ball due to each other?
question: Two billiard balls of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each moving in opposite directions with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collide and rebound with the same speed. If the time duration of contact is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>005</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, then what is the force exerted on the ball due to each other?
solution:
After collision, the balls change their directions of motion without changing the magnitudes of their velocity.

Change in momentum of any one ball

Δp→=2×0.05×10

⇒Δp→=1 kg m s-1

 The force exerted on the ball is F→av =Δp→Δt=10.005

Fav =200 N

q: A ball of mass 10 kg moving with a velocity 103 m s-1 along the x -axis, hits another ball of mass 20 kg which is at rest. After the collision, first ball comes to rest while the second ball disintegrates into two equal pieces. One piece starts moving along y -axis with a speed of 10 m s-1. The second piece starts moving at an angle of 30° with respect to the x -axis. The velocity of the ball moving at 30° with x -axis is x m s-1. The configuration of pieces after the collision is shown in the figure below. The value of x to the nearest integer is
question: A ball of mass 10 kg moving with a velocity 103 m s-1 along the x -axis, hits another ball of mass 20 kg which is at rest. After the collision, first ball comes to rest while the second ball disintegrates into two equal pieces. One piece starts moving along y -axis with a speed of 10 m s-1. The second piece starts moving at an angle of 30° with respect to the x -axis. The velocity of the ball moving at 30° with x -axis is x m s-1. The configuration of pieces after the collision is shown in the figure below. The value of x to the nearest integer is
solution:
Let the velocity of 2nd  fragment is v→ then by conservation of linear momentum
10(103)i^=(10)(10j^)+10v→
⇒v→=103i^-10j^
|v→|=300+100=400=20 m s-1.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Consider two blocks A and B of masses $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ that are placed on a frictionless table. The block A moves with a constant speed $v=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards the block B kept at rest. A spring with spring constant $\mathrm{k}=3000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is attached with the block B as shown in the figure. After the collision, suppose that the blocks A and B, along with the spring in constant compression state, move together, then the compression in the spring is, (Neglect the mass of the spring)
question: Consider two blocks A and B of masses $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ that are placed on a frictionless table. The block A moves with a constant speed $v=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards the block B kept at rest. A spring with spring constant $\mathrm{k}=3000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is attached with the block B as shown in the figure. After the collision, suppose that the blocks A and B, along with the spring in constant compression state, move together, then the compression in the spring is, (Neglect the mass of the spring)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2=\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right) \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}} \\ & \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}} \Rightarrow \frac{10 \times 3}{10+5} \Rightarrow \frac{30}{15}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^2=\frac{1}{2}(10)(3)^2-\left[\frac{1}{2}(15)(2)^2\right]$
$\Rightarrow 90-60=30=3000 x^2$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2 \Rightarrow \frac{30}{3000}=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m}.\end{aligned}$

question: A body of mass $m=3.513 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving along the $x$-axis with a speed of $5.00 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The magnitude of its momentum is recorded as
solution:
$$
P=m v=3.513 \times 5.00 \approx 17.6
$$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: In a perfectly inelastic collision, two spheres made of the same material with masses 15 kg and 25 kg, moving in opposite directions with speeds of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively, strike each other and stick together. The rise in temperature (in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$), if all the heat produced during the collision is retained by these spheres, is : (specific heat of sphere material $31 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{cal}=4.2 \mathrm{~J}$)
question: In a perfectly inelastic collision, two spheres made of the same material with masses 15 kg and 25 kg, moving in opposite directions with speeds of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively, strike each other and stick together. The rise in temperature (in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$), if all the heat produced during the collision is retained by these spheres, is : (specific heat of sphere material $31 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{cal}=4.2 \mathrm{~J}$)
solution:
Using conservation of momentum:
$m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 = (m_1 + m_2)v_f$ where $v_2 = -30$ m/s (opposite direction).
$15(10) + 25(-30) = 40v_f \Rightarrow v_f = -15$ m/s
Initial kinetic energy:
$KE_i = \frac{1}{2}(15)(10)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(25)(30)^2 = 750 + 11250 = 12000$ J
Final kinetic energy:
$KE_f = \frac{1}{2}(40)(15)^2 = 4500$ J
Heat produced:
$Q = 12000 - 4500 = 7500$ J $= \frac{7500}{4.2} = 1785.71$ cal
Temperature rise:
$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{m_{total} \cdot c} = \frac{1785.71}{40 \times 31} = 1.44°C$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A proton of mass $\mathrm{m}$ collides elastically with a particle of unknown mass at rest. After the collision, the proton and the unknown particle are seen moving at an angle of $90^{\circ}$ with respect to each other. The mass of unknown particle is:
question: A proton of mass $\mathrm{m}$ collides elastically with a particle of unknown mass at rest. After the collision, the proton and the unknown particle are seen moving at an angle of $90^{\circ}$ with respect to each other. The mass of unknown particle is:
solution:
Apply principle of conservation of momentum along $x$-direction, $$ \begin{aligned} m u &=m v_1 \cos 45^{\circ}+M v_2 \cos 45^{\circ} \\ m u &=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(m v_1+M v_2\right) \end{aligned} $$ Along $y$-direction, $$ \begin{aligned} &o=m v_1 \sin 45^{\circ}-M v_2 \sin 45^{\circ} \\ &o=\left(m v_1-M v_2\right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} $$
Coefficient of restution $e=1$ $$ =\frac{v_2-v_1 \cos 90}{u \cos 45} $$ ( $\because$ Collision is elastic) $$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{v_2}{\frac{u}{\sqrt{2}}}=1 \\ &\Rightarrow u=\sqrt{2} v_2 \end{aligned} $$ Solving eqs (i), (ii), \& (iii), we get mass of unknown particle, $M=m$

q: A large block of wood of mass M=5.99 kg is hanging from two long massless cords. A bullet of mass m=10 g is fired into the block and gets embedded in it. The (block + bullet) then swing upwards, their center of mass rising a vertical distance h=9.8 cm before the (block + bullet) pendulum comes momentarily to rest at the end of its arc. The speed of the bullet just before the collision is: (Take g=9.8 m s-2)
question: A large block of wood of mass M=5.99 kg is hanging from two long massless cords. A bullet of mass m=10 g is fired into the block and gets embedded in it. The (block + bullet) then swing upwards, their center of mass rising a vertical distance h=9.8 cm before the (block + bullet) pendulum comes momentarily to rest at the end of its arc. The speed of the bullet just before the collision is: (Take g=9.8 m s-2)
solution:
From energy conservation,

[after bullet gets embedded till the system comes momentarily at rest]

M+mgh=12M+mv12
[v1 is velocity after collision] 

∴ v1=2σh

Applying momentum conservation, (just before and just after collision)

mv=M+mv1

v=M+mmv1=610×10-3×2×9.8×9.8×10-2

≈831.55 m s-1

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform thin rod AB of length <math> <mi>L</mi> </math> has linear mass density <math><mi>μ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math> <mi>x</mi> </math> is measured from A. If the CM of the rod lies at a distance of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from A, then <math> <mi>a</mi> </math> and <math> <mi>b</mi> </math> are related as:
question: A uniform thin rod AB of length <math> <mi>L</mi> </math> has linear mass density <math><mi>μ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math> <mi>x</mi> </math> is measured from A. If the CM of the rod lies at a distance of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from A, then <math> <mi>a</mi> </math> and <math> <mi>b</mi> </math> are related as:
solution:
Consider an infinitesimal small element of length dx and mars density λ

xcm=∫dmx∫dm=∫λ dxx∫λ dx

=∫0La+bLxxdx∫0La+bLxdx

=∫0Laxdx+∫0LbLx2dx∫0Ladx+∫0LbLxdx

=ax220L+bL x330Lax0L+bLx220L

=a2L2-02+b3L L3-03aL-0+b2LL2-02

=a2L2+bL23aL+bL2=a2L+bL3a+b2

∵xcm=7L12=a2+b3La+b2⇒712= 3a+2b62a+b2

⇒712=3a+2b3×12a+b⇒72a+b=43a+2b

⇒14a+7b=12a+8b⇒2a=b

q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is at rest at the edge of a table, of height 1 m. A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> collides with the block and sticks to it. If the velocity of the bullet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the horizontal direction just before the collision then the kinetic energy just before the combined system strikes the floor, is [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Assume there is no rotational motion and loss of energy after the collision is negligible.]
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is at rest at the edge of a table, of height 1 m. A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> collides with the block and sticks to it. If the velocity of the bullet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the horizontal direction just before the collision then the kinetic energy just before the combined system strikes the floor, is [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Assume there is no rotational motion and loss of energy after the collision is negligible.]
solution:
Conservation of linear momentum

0.1×20=0.1+1.9×v

v=1 m s-1

Using work energy theorem

Wg=∆k

2×g×1=k-12×2×12

∴   k=21 J

q: A bomb of mass $16 \mathrm{~kg}$ at rest explodes into two pieces of masses of $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $12 \mathrm{~kg}$. The velocity of the $12 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is $4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The kinetic energy of the other mass is
question: A bomb of mass $16 \mathrm{~kg}$ at rest explodes into two pieces of masses of $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $12 \mathrm{~kg}$. The velocity of the $12 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is $4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The kinetic energy of the other mass is
solution:
$m_1 v_1=m_2 v_2$
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 144=288 \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Distance of the centre of mass of a solid uniform cone from its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the radius of its base is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and its height is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to:
question: Distance of the centre of mass of a solid uniform cone from its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the radius of its base is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and its height is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to:
solution:
Let the mass of solid cylinder is M and angle with vertical is θ. Now, we take a small element of thickness dy at a distance y from the vertex. The radius of small element is r, as shown in the figure below.

Then, tanθ=Rh=ry which will give, r=ytanθ. The density of the cone is given by,

ρ=M13πR2h=3MπR2h.

The mass of the small element is given by,

dM=ρdV

dM=3MπR2h×πr2dy=3My2tan2θR2hdy

The center of mass of the cone is given by,

z0=1M∫0hydM

z0=1M∫0hy·3My2tan2θR2hdy

z0=3R2hRh2y440h

z0=3h4.

q: A body having specific charge 8 μC g-1 is resting on a frictionless plane at a distance 10 cm from the wall (as shown in the figure). It starts moving towards the wall when a uniform electric field of 100 V m-1 is applied horizontally towards the wall. If the collision of the body with the wall is perfectly elastic, then the time period of the motion will be ____s.
question: A body having specific charge 8 μC g-1 is resting on a frictionless plane at a distance 10 cm from the wall (as shown in the figure). It starts moving towards the wall when a uniform electric field of 100 V m-1 is applied horizontally towards the wall. If the collision of the body with the wall is perfectly elastic, then the time period of the motion will be ____s.
solution:
F=ma
qE=ma
a=qEm
Now d=12at2
t=2 da
t=2 dqEm

t=2×0.18×10-610-3×100=12
∴ Time period =2t=1 s
Answer =1.00

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: In a collinear collision, a particle with an initial speed <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> strikes a stationary particle of the same mass. If the final total kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> greater than the original kinetic energy, the magnitude of the relative velocity between the two particles, after the collision, is
question: In a collinear collision, a particle with an initial speed <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> strikes a stationary particle of the same mass. If the final total kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> greater than the original kinetic energy, the magnitude of the relative velocity between the two particles, after the collision, is
solution:
KE2=32mv02. Conservation of momentum,  v 1 + v 2 = v 0 . Conservation of energy, 12mv12+12mv22=12 × 32mv02
⇒v12+v22=32v02⇒v12+v22+2v1v2=v02⇒32v02+2v1v2=v02
